speaker: interviewer
text: December 2nd, 1994, interview with Margot Heuman.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, the only thing I still want to say is after this accident and Erica's being killed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt that I really didn't need this, on top of it, which, which is probably self-pity. But on top of everything else, why did this have to happen to me, this-- you know, in addition to everything else. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people gave me these books written-- 'What Happens When Bad Things Happen to Good People,' which is-- and then the rabbi came to my bed in the hospital and all that. And really, all I wanted to do is be left alone and try to, you know, get over this. But Jill, Andy and Harris-- this is my youngest grandson-- came to Massachusetts, stayed in the house with me, and stayed with me while I was there as well as-- some of my friends took weeks off from work and came to stay with me. And the ski club was 100% behind me. I never-- the hospital has never-- had never seen so many cards and so many flowers in the history of the hospital in Pittsfield, Massachusetts. So it's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's, it can make one-- I feel good knowing that people do care. But it's something that I had to learn to live with. And life goes on. And the best thing that ever happened to me is my children and now my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have four grandchildren. And each one has-- they each had first a girl and then a boy. Jill has Dina and Harris.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Dan has-- Dina is nine, Harris is five. He just was five. And Dan has Lily and Jake. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dan's wife, Ursula, is Catholic. But the children do go-- are being brought up Jewish. They go to Hebrew school. And they have joined a temple. And even though Ursula did not convert, she does go to temple with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they know anything about the Holocaust yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandchildren? Very little. Maybe Lily might know a little, because my son is trying to push her into it. But I feel that when they are ready, they will ask questions. And I don't believe in upsetting the children by telling them anything now. I mean, it's up to them. But to have two children and have them turn out the kind of people that I've wanted them to be is enough for me. And that's the best. The worst was Erica. The best is my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: My will to live. I never gave up, ever, at any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get that from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. It's something being together with Dita, and we did it together. Now, we realize now, we talk about it-- because I see her. We meet at least once a year. Either I go to Toronto or else she comes to here. And she's married there. She has three children. She also has a bunch of grandchildren. Her husband is a pathologist. Neither one of us would have survived without the other. And we both realize that. Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we gave each other hope and support and love and friendship. And I think that's what makes life worth, worth living. And this is why my experiences are probably not very similar to other people's. Because even though it was absolute hell, what I remember most is my friendship and love for Dita, of all those years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what message do you have about all this to the world, to your grandchildren, that you'd like to express?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think you have to have it within yourself. And whatever comes from the outside is secondary. And you can cope with it, if you have-- if you have someone you care for, if you have certain principles that you abide by, and if you do what you feel is right and not what the world tells you to do. I think that's more important than anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has the world learned, do you think, from all of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The world will never learn. As a matter of fact, I was in Germany. And when I was there in October, my cousin's daughter-- who by the way is very pro-Jewish, has been in Israel and is leaning toward Judaism, even though she was brought up Catholic-- asked me the one thing I've-- what advice would I give her more than anything else. And I had to quote Shakespeare, and I just told her, 'To thine own self be true.' Because I think that's more important than anything. I mean, if, if you don't do-- in order to be a person, a whole person, you have to be you. You can't be what anybody else wants you to be. And I try to live my life like that, and I hope that my children will.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's take the break now and go to the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Starting now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This picture was taken in 1932. And it's my little sister, my mother, and I. And it was taken in Hellenthal. It was like a family portrait.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell me your mother's full name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother's name was Johanna Heumann. My sister's name was Lore. And then my name, Margo, which is--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1935. And it's my sister, who was Lore, who was three years old at the time. And I was about five. And at the time, we lived in Lippstadt. We just had moved there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Left to-- starting with-- say left to right on this. Tell me who these people are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Left, that's starting from here, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This picture was taken in 1940. And it was taken in Bielefeld, the last place we lived. And it's my sister, Lore, my father, my mother, and I'm at the other end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on one moment. I have to do this. I have to point towards the feet, too, so we see what shoes people wore then. There's the date in it. Hold on. Then the full, wide picture. OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo that was taken in Sweden. I believe it was the end, probably December, '45. And this little-- the little girl is a niece of the woman, Mrs. Dandenell that I lived with. And the young girl is me, the young woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is Marta Dandenell, the woman who took me into her home and educated me, and I lived with in Sweden. Great person.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll do the full picture. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Approximately what year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was probably taken about 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, this was during-- she gave you this picture after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she gave me the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked for picture. Great way to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I should be doing. That's how my parents met.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on. Go ahead. You can start.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture is of Dita, taken in England in 1950. I guess she had graduated and was a nurse at that point. She was also a midwife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one second.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't worry about that. I'll put those back. No problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a photograph taken in 1959. And there's Jill and Dan on my lap. And I'm in the background. And Dan is about two and Jill is about five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one second. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] All right. Go ahead and begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken at Dan's bar mitzvah in 1970. And the little guy is Uncle Max, who lived to be over 100. He is my husband's uncle. And my mother-in-law, who is also dead now, Lillian. And I'm in the middle. And then there's Dan, there's Charles, my husband. Then there's Jill.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, just one second, because I'm just going to do a left to right. There is little Max.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he walked over the Brooklyn Bridge, the day it opened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And then he told the kids about World War I, all these stories. They used to love him. I mean my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's terrific.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now he was-- they were born in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all were, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a beautiful picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go right ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a photo taken in Toronto in 1978, while I was visiting with Dita. And Dita is sitting below, and I'm above her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, just a second. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK, there's Dita. Dita below, and Margo above.

speaker: interviewee
text: She'd come in, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever you're ready, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo that was taken at our ski lodge in Waitsfield, Vermont. And it's a work weekend, which we have every summer, to keep the lodge in tip-top shape. This is Erica. And it was taken about 1988.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- my retirement photo, which was taken about 1989. It's a party that was given for me by my employer. And it's Dan, Jill, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you can-- why don't you introduce me to your daughter here. Who is--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me who is--

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This is my daughter, Jill. And I have spoken about Jill on the videotape before. And she's my oldest child. JILL

speaker: interviewee
text: Jill. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you don't remember it, Jill, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Remember what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother described how one of the happiest days of her life was giving birth to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you're right. I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you heard this story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Although I believe I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you heard-- right. Have you heard this kind of story from her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And the extension of it was that that meant that Hitler did not succeed. So I have heard that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, didn't you feel the same way, more or less, when Dina was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably not to the degree that you felt it, but certainly the notion of continuity is there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What has your mother's experiences meant to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean it's interesting, which we talked about just before you started filming this, is that I don't know a lot of her experience. I don't know a lot of her experience. A lot of what I've heard has been from other people, other survivors who I know or have known throughout my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's this big emotional block inside me that doesn't really know the details about it, but just, in a general sense, the experience based on some of the reading I've done and others' experiences who've shared some of it with me. So it means a lot, obviously. It's affected my life in different ways. What has your mother taught you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rely on yourself. [LAUGHS] A lesson my brother has learned, as well. Well, I felt without-- that's the only chance. That was my chance for survival, is that it's up to you, basically. And I think it's something that I hope you teach your children, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hm And not so much that she's taught me, but just observing her life, that although I'm agnostic, I sometimes truly believe in the notion of angels, because she's had, in some respects, a very protected life, despite the Holocaust. The fact that she survived and she's lived through some additional, very terrible experiences, has come out the other side. So I really think that she may, in fact, have a guardian angel at some point, looking out for her. Although it's exhibited in strange ways, by the fact that she survived the Holocaust but had to go through it. I believe that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also talk about she passed down to you her family's love for skiing and hiking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, expensive sports, skiing and hiking. [LAUGHS] Unfortunately, with children it's hard to do. But the outdoors, yeah, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you actually-- you work in, in the Jewish community, don't you. Can you say what you do and maybe a word or two about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I work at United Jewish Appeal, Federation of Jewish Philanthropies. I've been there for 10 years now. I'm in the program services department. I direct a program that works with consultants who provide pro bono services to the agencies in New York who get funding from UJA Federation. I also do some fund raising. And it's very personally rewarding and meaningful to me to be there, one of the reasons I've been there for 10 years. And I enjoy it very much. It really gives purpose to my life in many respects.

speaker: interviewee
text: Partially, Jill's father was the kind of person that believed that one should work for nonprofit organizations and was more or less anti-establishment. And I am very happy that both my children are working for nonprofit organizations, to some extent my son. Because what I actually did in advertising, even though I loved it, it was really absolutely meaningless. And this is something I think I also tried to teach my children, that it really didn't mean anything at all and to do something worthwhile, if at all possible, with your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but the added piece for me about being in the Jewish community is that I had worked in the nonprofit, nonsectarian sector. And this has an additional meaning for me, because it's for the Jewish community. And even though it's not something in terms of my observance, personally it's meaningful to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, does this-- is there any connection between your [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] appreciation of the nonprofit sectors-- you're saying hoping that your children would go into this work-- and your own experience in the war and your own philosophy of life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just feel that life is very short, and one should do something where someone else will benefit, which is-- there's certainly no benefit for anyone in advertising except monetary rewards. But if you could help someone, I think that's a great thing to do. And this is why I have been volunteering, too, now, since I have free time. And I'm doing things to help people when I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the volunteering you said you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I work for the Big Apple Greeter, which I love. And I should have been a tour guide, but it's too late for that now. But I have been taking people, basically foreign people, who visit New York City for the first time. And I show them Manhattan, because I really love Manhattan. I'm also in a senior aide group now, where I have an old person that I visit once a week and spend like an hour, two hours with. And this is someone who doesn't have a family and really has no contact with the outside world, except for people like me. And I volunteer for all kinds of-- do volunteer work for a lot of sports organizations. But basically, I feel that I have to do something to help someone else, and I think that's important. And I think Jill is doing this in her job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Along with your swimming three times a week and all the outdoor stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: At least three times a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say three? I'd say five to seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I'm-- when I'm in the city, I swim every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? Where do you swim?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the Manhattan Plaza Health Club, where I have a senior membership. But it's not very expensive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wish I had that. That's great. How much do you swim each time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I swim for half an hour, which is about 3/4 of a mile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I wish I could be in the same shape. [LAUGHS] Not. So that's-- that's it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you going to share this with your children at some point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, I don't know if my mother told you, we tried to sort of do a oral history with a video camera, couple of years ago. But I think Dina was too little and the camera equipment wasn't that great. And she kept interrupting us. And it just didn't come out, so we were going to do it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is perfect. So we don't have to do it again. You guys have done it for us. So yeah.
